Jesus teaches His disciples that prayer is not a luxury for the spiritually ambitious—it is a basic necessity for those who follow Him. The widow who never stops asking the unjust judge for justice represents those who cry out to God day and night. But here is what makes this parable sting: if an ungodly judge will finally give justice simply because he is worn out by persistence, how much more will the perfectly righteous God respond to His chosen people? The shock is not that we must keep praying; the shock is that we often stop. Jesus presses further with a question that cuts through centuries of confident Christianity: "When the Son of Man comes, will He find faith on earth?" He is asking whether we will still be praying, still be trusting, still be waiting for His justice when He returns. The assumption embedded in His question is that many will have given up. Many will have lost heart.

Immediately after this, Jesus turns to expose the prayer that does not pray at all. The Pharisee stands in the temple congratulating himself before God—counting his disciplines, comparing himself favorably to others, essentially thanking God that he is not like that tax collector over there. The tax collector, meanwhile, cannot even look upward. His only word is a confession: "God, have mercy on me, a sinner." This is not the prayer of someone keeping score. This is the prayer of someone who knows he is undone. And Jesus declares that this man goes home justified before God, not the one who came with a resume of righteousness. The principle is absolute: those who lift themselves up will be brought down, and those who humble themselves will be lifted up. The question is whether we recognize ourselves in either of these men, and which one we most resemble when we actually pray.

The rich ruler who asks about eternal life believes he has earned his way through moral compliance. He has kept the commandments since youth. But Jesus names what he lacks: everything. When Jesus tells him to sell all he owns and follow, the man's face falls because his wealth has become his god. He cannot imagine that following Jesus means releasing what he thinks secures his life. This is why Jesus says it is harder for the rich to enter the kingdom than for a camel to squeeze through a needle's eye—not because money is evil, but because money convinces us we do not need God's mercy. Yet Jesus does not soften the demand. Peter's boast that he has left everything draws a promise: those who have abandoned what this world calls security will receive it back multiplied, both in this age and in the age to come. The cost of following Jesus is total. The return is infinite.

As Jesus marches toward Jerusalem, He again tells His disciples plainly what awaits Him—mockery, insults, flogging, death, and resurrection on the third day. But the disciples understand nothing. The meaning is hidden from them. They cannot yet see that this death is not a tragedy but the very heart of redemption. Then, on the road near Jericho, a blind beggar does what the disciples cannot: he recognizes Jesus and cries out in faith. When the crowd tries to silence him, he shouts louder. Jesus stops for him, asks what he needs, and grants him sight. The blind man's response is immediate—he follows Jesus and glorifies God. All the people praise God too. The irony is unmissable. The disciples, who walk with Jesus daily, are spiritually blind. The beggar, who cannot see at all, sees exactly who Jesus is and responds with the faith that Jesus has been asking for all along. Faith is not about understanding everything. It is about recognizing the Messiah when He passes by and crying out for mercy without hesitation.
